title: Acoustic Emission Testing: Fundamentals and Sources
narrationScript: Acoustic emission testing inverts the usual NDT bargain: instead of putting energy into the part, you stress the structure and let active flaws announce themselves. A crack jump, a snapping fiber, or a burst of dislocation motion releases stored elastic energy as a transient stress wave. Piezoelectric sensors, most resonant near one hundred fifty kilohertz, catch it; preamplifiers boost and filter it; and a multichannel system reduces every hit to amplitude, counts, duration, rise time, and energy, then locates the source from arrival-time differences between sensors. Two effects govern test design. Kaiser: a sound structure stays silent on reloading until its previous maximum stress is exceeded, because emission is a one-shot phenomenon. Felicity: damaged structures, especially fiber-reinforced plastics, speak early, and a Felicity ratio below one flags significant damage. You will also learn the source families, from crack growth and plastic deformation to composite fiber breaks, the noise sources you must defeat, and the pencil-lead break that proves every channel is alive. AE locates active flaws; other methods size them.

sections.heading: The Method That Listens
sections.body:
Most NDT methods interrogate a part: a transducer, a coil, or a radiation source puts energy in, and the instrument reads what comes back. Acoustic emission testing (AE) is the **passive** exception. Stress a structure, and active degradation inside it — a crack front jumping ahead, dislocations breaking loose, a fiber snapping — releases stored elastic energy. ASTM E1316 defines acoustic emission as **transient elastic waves generated by the rapid release of energy from localized sources within a material**. Those waves spread through the structure to sensors fixed on its surface. The structure is the transmitter; the instrument only listens.

Three consequences define the method:

- **A stimulus is required.** Emission appears only while the structure is being stressed — a controlled pressurization, a proof load, thermal gradients. A flaw that does not move under the applied stress is silent.
- **Only active sources speak.** AE separates a growing crack from a dormant one in a single test — the indications it produces are, by definition, the ones doing damage right now.
- **Coverage is global.** A handful of fixed sensors monitors an entire vessel, tank, or bridge member in real time, welds and inaccessible regions included, with no scanning.

People heard acoustic emission long before instruments existed: bend a bar of tin and it crackles audibly as the crystal lattice twins — the 'tin cry' known for centuries. The modern method dates to **Josef Kaiser's 1950 research** in Munich, which put instrumentation on the phenomenon and uncovered the load-memory effect that carries his name.

sections.caption: The AE signal path: a crack jump releases stored elastic energy as a stress wave; the sensor converts surface motion into a voltage, the preamplifier boosts and filters it, and the system reduces each burst to amplitude, counts, duration, and energy.

sections.heading: Burst, Continuous, and What the System Measures
sections.body:
AE arrives in two characters. **Burst emission** is a discrete, decaying transient from a discrete event — a crack jump, an inclusion fracturing, a fiber break. **Continuous emission** is a sustained elevation of the background — thousands of small events overlapping — typical of plastic deformation and of leaks. Practical AE works roughly between **20 kHz and 1 MHz**: above most mechanical background noise, below crippling attenuation. Most structural tests use sensors resonant near **150 kHz**, in a working band of about 100–300 kHz.

The system reduces each detected transient — each **hit** — to a handful of features measured against a preset detection **threshold**:

- **Peak amplitude** — on a decibel scale referenced to 1 microvolt at the sensor; the strongest single discriminator between source types
- **Counts** (ringdown counts) — the number of times the signal crosses the threshold within the hit
- **Duration** and **rise time** — first crossing to last, and first crossing to peak; friction and electromagnetic spikes betray themselves here
- **Energy (MARSE)** — the measured area of the rectified signal envelope, less sensitive than counts to the exact threshold setting

Because every channel time-stamps its hits, **arrival-time differences (ΔT)** between sensors place the source: two sensors give **linear location** along a pipe, three or more **triangulate** a position on a shell, and **zone location** simply credits the hit to the nearest sensor. The working displays are cumulative counts or energy against load, amplitude distributions, and location maps.

sections.heading: Load Memory: The Kaiser and Felicity Effects
sections.body:
Kaiser's discovery still anchors AE test design. Load a sound metal specimen and it emits; unload and reload it, and it stays **silent until the previous maximum stress is exceeded**. ASTM E1316 defines the **Kaiser effect** as the absence of detectable acoustic emission until previously applied stress levels are exceeded. The mechanism is bookkeeping: emission is **irreversible**. Every dislocation jump and micro-fracture spends its stored energy exactly once, so the damage that could happen below a given stress has already happened. The structure remembers its highest load.

Two practical rules follow. First, to make a structure speak, the test load must climb meaningfully above recent service loads — which is why AE test pressures sit above normal operating pressure. Second, data not captured is gone: you cannot replay the emission by re-running the test.

The **Felicity effect** is the diagnostically valuable breakdown of that memory: in damaged structures — most prominently in **fiber-reinforced plastic (FRP)** vessels and tanks — emission returns at loads *below* the previous maximum. The **Felicity ratio** is the load at which emission reappears divided by the previous maximum load. A ratio of about **1.0 or higher** means the Kaiser effect held: the structure is behaving as if sound. A ratio **below 1.0** means damage is progressing, and the lower the ratio, the more significant it is — the basis of acceptance criteria in FRP vessel examination. A related red flag: **emission that continues during a constant-load hold**. A stable structure falls silent under steady load; one that keeps talking is actively degrading.

sections.caption: Load memory in two panels: a sound structure reloads in silence until the previous maximum P1 is exceeded (Kaiser effect); a damaged one emits early at load F, and a Felicity ratio F ÷ P1 below 1 grades the damage.

sections.heading: Where Emissions Come From
sections.body:
**Primary sources** release energy from the material's own microstructure as it degrades:

- **Crack initiation and growth** — fatigue, stress-corrosion cracking, hydrogen-induced cracking: every crack-front advance is a burst, which makes AE a natural monitor for pressure vessels and structures in service
- **Plastic deformation** — the collective motion of dislocations; low-amplitude continuous emission, strongest as the material passes through yield
- **Twinning** — abrupt lattice reorientation; the audible 'tin cry'
- **Phase transformation** — martensite forming during quenching or weld cooling arrives as bursts
- **Fracture and decohesion of inclusions** — manganese sulfide inclusions in steel are classic early-load emitters

**Composites** are among AE's most productive targets because every damage mechanism emits: **matrix cracking** (numerous, lower amplitude), **fiber–matrix debonding**, **delamination**, **fiber pullout**, and **fiber breakage** (sparse, high amplitude). Amplitude sorting alone goes a long way toward telling them apart.

**Secondary (pseudo) sources** release energy mechanically rather than from degradation of the bulk material: crack-face **rubbing** under changing load, cracking of brittle **oxide or mill scale**, **slag cracking** on cooling welds, and **leaks**, whose turbulence radiates continuous broadband energy — a nuisance during flaw monitoring and the entire basis of AE leak detection.

The method's permanent enemy is **noise**: pumps and valves, rain on a shell, slings and supports rubbing, grinding nearby, electromagnetic interference. The defenses are frequency filtering, threshold setting, **guard sensors** that veto hits arriving from outside the array, and quiet, controlled loading.

sections.caption: Common AE sources and their signal character.
rows: Discrete higher-amplitude hits, locatable by ΔT — the classic AE target
Low-amplitude sustained emission, strongest near yield
Sharp hits; audible in tin, present in titanium and zirconium alloys
Inclusion fracture or decohesion (MnS in steel)
Hits early in loading as brittle particles crack or separate
Composite damage: matrix crack to fiber break
Amplitude climbs with severity — matrix cracking low, fiber breakage high
Leakage through a crack or fitting
Steady broadband signal — noise in flaw tests, the signal in leak detection
Secondary source — long rise times help unmask it; filter or guard it out

sections.heading: The Measurement Chain: Sensor to System
sections.body:
Everything in the chain exists to catch microvolts of surface motion and turn them into decisions.

- **Sensors.** Piezoelectric elements — lead zirconate titanate (PZT) is standard. **Resonant** sensors ring at one frequency (the ~150 kHz workhorse) and buy maximum sensitivity; **broadband** sensors trade sensitivity for a flat response used in waveform and frequency analysis.
- **Coupling and mounting.** Like UT, AE needs a **couplant** — grease or adhesive — plus a steady hold-down: magnetic clamps, bands, or bonded mounts. On hot or inaccessible surfaces, a metal **waveguide** rod carries the wave out to a cooler place where the sensor can live.
- **Preamplifier.** Sensor outputs are tiny, so a preamplifier sits at — often inside — the sensor, typically providing **40 dB of gain** plus bandpass filtering, and drives the long cable to the processor.
- **Processor and displays.** The multichannel system applies the threshold, time-stamps hits, extracts the features, computes ΔT locations, and plots the working displays — cumulative activity against load, amplitude distributions, location maps — with alarms for hot zones.
- **Verification.** Before the test counts, every channel is proven with a **Hsu-Nielsen source**: snap a **0.3 or 0.5 mm 2H pencil lead**, extended about 3 mm, against the surface near each sensor through a guide shoe. The break releases a reproducible simulated burst that verifies coupling and channel sensitivity per ASTM E976.
- **The material sets the array.** Attenuation governs sensor spacing — fine-grained steel carries waves many meters, while attenuative FRP needs a much denser array. In thin shells the wave travels as plate (Lamb) modes whose velocity depends on mode and frequency, and the location setup must account for that.

sections.caption: A working AE test: controlled pressurization stresses the vessel, the source's wavefront reaches the nearest sensor first, and the multichannel system turns arrival-time differences into a source location for follow-up inspection by other methods.

figures.title: Source location on a pressure vessel

interactive.title: First Hit Wins
interactive.intro: During a pressure hold, the flaw fires one burst and every channel is time-stamped. Click the component that records the FIRST hit.

regions.label: Sensor S2
regions.feedback: Correct — S2 sits closest to the source, so the wavefront reaches it first. The location processor pairs that first hit with the later arrivals at S3 and S1 to triangulate the flaw.

regions.label: Sensor S3
regions.feedback: S3 hears the same burst, but second — the wave travels farther to reach it. Its arrival-time difference relative to S2 is exactly what the location algorithm feeds on.

regions.label: Sensor S1
regions.feedback: S1 is the farthest sensor, so it records the burst last — and in a high-attenuation material it might miss it entirely. That is why attenuation sets sensor spacing.

regions.label: Preamplifiers
regions.feedback: The preamplifier boosts whatever the sensors deliver — it amplifies hits, it does not detect them. Detection happens at the sensor face.

regions.label: Active flaw
regions.feedback: The flaw is the source, not a detector — it converts stored elastic energy into the stress wave every sensor hears. Its position is the answer the system computes.

checkQuestions.question: Per ASTM E1316, acoustic emission is best described as:
options: Ultrasonic energy introduced into the part by a transducer and reflected from internal flaws
Transient elastic waves generated by the rapid release of energy from localized sources within a material
Electromagnetic interference radiated by stressed metallic structures
Audible structure-borne noise produced by loose parts and machinery
checkQuestions.explanation: AE is a passive method: the stressed material itself releases stored elastic energy as transient stress waves. Methods that introduce energy and read reflections — like UT — are active; AE only listens.

checkQuestions.question: A structurally sound steel vessel is pressurized, depressurized, then repressurized. According to the Kaiser effect, detectable emission on the second loading should:
options: Begin immediately, since flaws emit at any stress level
Begin at exactly half the previous maximum pressure
Be absent until the previous maximum stress level is exceeded
Occur only during depressurization
checkQuestions.explanation: Kaiser's 1950 discovery: emission is irreversible. The energy releasable below the previous maximum was spent on the first loading, so a sound structure stays quiet until that level is exceeded.

checkQuestions.question: During requalification of an FRP tank, significant emission begins at 70 percent of the previous maximum load. The Felicity ratio is:
options: 0.70 — below 1.0, indicating structurally significant damage
1.43 — above 1.0, indicating a sound structure
0.70 — below 1.0, which confirms the Kaiser effect held
Indeterminate without the total ringdown count
checkQuestions.explanation: Felicity ratio = load at emission onset ÷ previous maximum = 0.70. A ratio below 1.0 means the Kaiser effect broke down — emission returned early — which marks progressing damage; the lower the ratio, the more severe it is.

checkQuestions.question: Which source characteristically produces continuous emission rather than discrete burst signals?
options: A fatigue crack advancing through a weld
Fiber breakage in a composite overwrap
Deformation twinning in tin
Plastic deformation by dislocation motion near yield
checkQuestions.explanation: Crack jumps, fiber breaks, and twinning are discrete events — bursts. The overlapping motion of enormous numbers of dislocations merges into low-amplitude, sustained continuous emission, as does turbulent leakage.

checkQuestions.question: Immediately before an AE test, the operator breaks a 0.3 mm 2H pencil lead against the vessel wall near each sensor. The purpose is to:
options: Calibrate the pressure transducer used for the load schedule
Inject a reproducible simulated AE burst that verifies sensor coupling and channel response
Measure the vessel material's fracture toughness in place
Establish the Felicity ratio before loading begins
checkQuestions.explanation: That is the Hsu-Nielsen source: a lead break of specified diameter, hardness, and extension produces a repeatable burst. Per ASTM E976 it proves each sensor is coupled and each channel responds at the expected sensitivity — it says nothing about the load system or the structure's damage state.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Basic Examination Topical Outline §2.1.1 (AE fundamentals: principles/theory, sources of acoustic emission, equipment and material)
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations — Section B, Acoustic Emission (definitions of acoustic emission, Kaiser effect, Felicity ratio)
ASTM E976, Standard Guide for Determining the Reproducibility of Acoustic Emission Sensor Response (Hsu-Nielsen pencil-lead break)
ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section V, Articles 11–13 (AE examination of FRP vessels, metallic vessels during pressure testing, and continuous monitoring)
